narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your undergrad work and LSAT prep, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd like—landing at a 46% chance of not passing, which tipped the scales against admission. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 1 grades placing you in the 3rd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest red flag, suggesting an early struggle to hit the ground running in a demanding legal environment. That was compounded by your LSAT score of 36, which, while solid for many, signaled to the model a potential hurdle in mastering the intense analytical demands ahead. Your Year 3 decile of 1st—meaning you climbed to the very top—showed real grit and improvement, but it wasn't quite enough to fully offset those earlier stumbles in the model's view. Layered on top was your lower-middle family income bracket, which often correlates with added pressures that can make the marathon of law school tougher to finish strong. Your impressive 3.7 undergrad GPA pulled in the right direction but couldn't overpower the rest, much like how being white and female didn't give the boosts the model sometimes sees in other profiles. Choosing part-time status, while practical for balancing life, nudged things further toward risk, as the model links it to divided focus. Together, these pieces painted a picture of someone with undeniable potential and upward momentum, but starting from a shakier foundation than most who clear the bar—it's not a judgment on your heart or hustle, just a cautious call to protect spots for those whose profiles historically align better with success. If you build on that late surge in grades and tackle those early weaknesses head-on, another school might see your story differently next time. Hang in there—you've already shown you can rise.
